steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.1.2
Test Case Name: Multi-turn Bot Response for Repetitive Tasks
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot utilizes a Task Module (pop-up modal) to collect repetitive or complex data (Name, DOB, Place, Designation) instead of long multi-turn chat.
For Non-Integrated accounts, check whether the bot either:
Sends multiple chat questions OR Opens a Task Module
If neither happens and the bot gives only one clear guidance message, the scenario is still PASS.
Updated Logic:
Before all steps, the agent must first check supported bot commands under “View prompts” from the compose tray and run all available commands to review app behavior.
If no commands are found, proceed with the original steps.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Check Supported Commands First:
Open “View prompts” from the compose tray.
Run all suggested commands that relate to workflows requiring multiple data inputs.
Observe the bot’s behavior (Task Module vs. multi-turn chat).
If No Commands Are Available:
Proceed with the below logic:
Trigger Workflow:
Initiate a feature that requires multiple data points (e.g., “Create Profile,” “Register User”).
Monitor Interaction Method:
Check if the bot opens a Task Module or sends a series of individual chat questions.
Evaluate Efficiency:
Confirm the Task Module allows entering all fields (Name, DOB, Place, Designation) in one modal interface.
Check Redundancy:
Ensure the bot does not revert to unnecessary multi-turn chat when data can be collected in UI.
Validate Non-Integrated Behavior:
For Non-Integrated accounts, multiple chat questions or Task Module are both acceptable.
If neither happens and the bot sends only one guidance message, count the result as PASS.
Expected Result:
The bot must open a Task Module for repetitive data fields in integrated scenarios.
For Non-Integrated accounts, multiple chat questions or a Task Module are acceptable.
A single clear guidance message is also valid.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The bot asked 4 separate questions in the chat thread in an integrated scenario. Suggested correction: Use a Task Module to collect Name, DOB, Place, and Designation in one form.” “Suggested command from View Prompts triggered multi-turn chat instead of Task Module.”
Evidence:
[Insert Screenshot of chat vs. Task Module]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.1
Test Case Name: Bot Response Card Action Buttons Redirecting Outside Teams
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that any action button inside a bot card that redirects to an external website/browser clearly indicates the redirection in the button text (e.g., “View in…”, “Open in…”, “This way to…”).
Updated Logic:
If the bot shares a bare hyperlink only (e.g., https://example.com) with no descriptive text/context, mark WARNING (not PASS/FAIL).
If no external indicator exists on links but a hyperlink is present and hovering changes the cursor to a hand icon, mark WARNING with:
“There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link.”
Additional Scope Rule: If the warning condition is observed in only one instance (e.g., a Task Module button lacks external signage) and other potential instances (e.g., hyperlinks in Help response) are not found, the warning message must reflect scope:
“Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found.”
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (ENFORCED ORDER)
1) Welcome Message Content:
Trigger or reset to show the Welcome message. Inspect all text, buttons, and hyperlinks in the Welcome message. For every hyperlink/text link: hover and note if the cursor becomes a hand icon. Check if external indicators exist in the label (e.g., “Open in Browser”, “View in Web Portal”, ↗️). Record any bare links or links without external indicators.
2) View Prompts (Compose Tray) & Run All Commands
Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray. type all listed commands under view prompts and capture each response (text/cards/errors). Skip running Help command if it listed under view prompts. For each response, inspect for hyperlinks, do the hover check, and verify label indicators.
3) Help Command & Actionables
 type help → confirm in‑app reply (message/card). Extract actionables/supported commands from the Help response: Buttons/CTAs, explicitly listed commands, deep‑link actions. Note any hyperlinks for later hover check. type all of the Help‑listed commands (one by one) → trigger each button/command exactly as shown and capture the response. Check links in each response: Hover → hand cursor? Label shows external indicator (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️)?
4) Generic Commands
Send “hi” and “hello”. Analyze responses for hyperlinks (hover test) and presence/absence of external indicators.
5) One Invalid Command
Send one invalid input (e.g., “xyz123”). Analyze response for hyperlinks (hover test) and indicators.
Across all steps (1–5):
If a bare hyperlink is found → WARNING. If a hyperlink shows hand cursor on hover but no external indicator in label → WARNING with the specified message. If such cases appear in any of one step, use the scoped warning text indicating other instances were not found. Collect and list all steps where hyperlinks with hand‑cursor behavior were detected.
Expected Result
Buttons redirecting outside Teams must include descriptive text. Hyperlinks showing a hand cursor on hover are eligible for review: If label has external indicator → contributes to PASS. If no indicator or bare link → WARNING.
Raw standalone links and links without descriptive indicators must be flagged as WARNING. When the issue is observed in only one instance, the WARNING must indicate that other instances were not found.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
PASS – Buttons/hyperlinks clearly indicate external navigation (labels such as “Open in Browser”, “View in Web Portal”, ↗️ present). FAIL – Buttons redirect externally without descriptive labeling. WARNING – Bare/raw hyperlink with no context, or Hyperlink present with no external indicator but hand‑cursor appears on hover → Use: “There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link.” Scoped case (only one instance found): Use: “Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found.”
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The ‘More Info’ button redirects externally with no indication. Suggested fix: rename to ‘Open in Browser’ or ‘View in Web Portal’.”
Warning Examples:
“Bot returned only a raw hyperlink without any context.” “There is a hyperlink added in bot response. Please verify the link.” “Warning captured for only one instance. Other instances (e.g., links in Help response) were not found.”
Hyperlink Detection Log (List All Steps Where Found):
Step 1 (Welcome): [quote/describe the hyperlink label/placement] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 2 (Help): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 3 (view Prompts command: ): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 4 (Generic): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No] Step 5 (Invalid): [details] — hover → hand cursor, indicator present? [Yes/No]
Evidence:
[Insert screenshots: Welcome message, Help response, prompts list & command responses, generic/invalid responses, and hover state with pointer hand visible]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.11
Test Case Name: Error Message for Invalid Commands
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the bot handles unrecognized or invalid inputs gracefully by providing a helpful response (such as a help menu or a list of valid commands) rather than remaining silent ("dead‑ending") or displaying a technical error message. For Non‑Integrated accounts, verify that the bot does not return an error and instead provides a way‑forward guidance message; this should be treated as a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Initiate Interaction: Open the chat interface with the bot.
Send Invalid Command: Type a random or unsupported string (e.g., "xyz123" or "invalid_command") and send it.
Observe Response: Monitor the bot's reaction to the unrecognized input.
Verify Content: Check if the response includes guidance such as a help card, suggested commands, or a polite clarification message.
Check for Dead Ends: Ensure the bot does not fail to reply or show technical errors. For Non‑Integrated accounts, confirm that a way‑forward guidance message is shown instead of an error—this is PASS.
Expected Result:
The bot must provide a constructive response to any invalid command. It must not display raw system errors or leave the user without next steps. For Non‑Integrated accounts, a single way‑forward guidance message is valid and should pass.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot did not respond to an invalid command, leaving the user in a dead‑end state. Suggested correction: Implement a fallback intent with guidance."
Example: "The bot returned a technical error message. Suggested correction: Replace error logs with user‑friendly messaging."
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot showing the invalid input and the bot’s response]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.12
Test Case Name: Providing Same Response to Both Valid and Invalid Commands

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the bot distinguishes between valid and invalid commands with specific, context‑aware responses. The bot should not trigger its default “Help” or “Fallback” response for recognized commands.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot gives the same response for both valid and invalid commands but includes a clear way‑forward guidance message, treat as WARNING (not Pass/Fail).

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (ENFORCED ORDER)
1) View Prompts — Discover & Run All Supported Commands

Click View prompts / View suggestions in the compose tray.
Run every listed command and record each response (text/cards/CTAs/intent).
Save the command → response pairs for comparison.

2) Help Command (Conditional)

If “Help” is listed in View Prompts → skip this step.
Otherwise, send help and record the response.
If the Help response mentions supported actions/commands (buttons/CTAs/listed commands), capture those items (names/labels/IDs) for Step 3.

3) Run Supported Actions Listed in Help (If Present)

Execute all supported actions/commands surfaced in the Help response (buttons/CTAs/listed commands).
Record each response.
Save the action → response pairs for comparison.

4) Generic Command

Send one generic input (e.g., hi or hello).
Record the response.

5) Invalid Command

Send one invalid input (e.g., xyz123).
Record the response.


Comparison & Decision Logic (APPLY EXACTLY AS BELOW)

Similarity Rule: “Same response” means materially identical text/cards/intent/CTAs (minor timestamp or message ID differences do not matter).

A) WARNING Condition

If any two responses among Steps 1–4 (Prompts, Help* if executed, Help‑listed actions, Generic) are the same,
→ WARNING with this message:
“Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message.”
(Examples: generic = same as prompts; prompts = same as help‑listed action; etc.)

B) FAIL Condition

If the Invalid command (Step 5) response matches any response from Steps 1–4,
→ FAIL and list explicitly which commands matched (e.g., “Invalid ‘xyz123’ matched response of ‘Check Status’ and ‘Create Task’”).
(This indicates valid and invalid are treated identically.)

C) PASS Condition

If no WARNING condition is triggered and no FAIL condition is triggered,
→ PASS (valid commands are distinct and invalid gets a fallback/guidance that does not duplicate valid/generic/help responses).


Note (Non‑Integrated accounts):
If the bot returns the same way‑forward guidance for both valid and invalid inputs, classify per above logic:

If Step 5 (invalid) matches any of Steps 1–4 → FAIL
Else if any matches within Steps 1–4 → WARNING
Else PASS


Expected Result:

Valid command(s) → trigger correct workflows/actions.
Invalid command → receives fallback/guidance that is not identical to any valid/generic/help/help‑listed action response.
Warnings flag sameness among valid/generic/help/help‑listed action buckets (Steps 1–4).
Fail flags sameness between invalid (Step 5) and any of Steps 1–4.


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Guidelines:

PASS

Distinct responses across: prompts commands, help/help‑listed actions (if run), generic;
Invalid command response differs from all others.


FAIL

Invalid (Step 5) response matches any response from Steps 1–4 (name all commands/actions that matched), or a valid command incorrectly triggers fallback/help.


WARNING

Any two or more among Steps 1–4 produce the same response.
Use: “Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message.”



Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):

FAIL Example: “Invalid ‘xyz123’ produced the same response as ‘Check Status’ and ‘Create Task’.”
WARNING Example: “Responses for ‘hi’ and ‘Check Status’ are identical. Same configuration/setup message received for the commands. Kindly review the message.”

Evidence:
Attach screenshots of:

View prompts list
Each prompted command execution & response
Help response (if executed) and help‑listed actions (if any) & their responses
Generic command response
Invalid command response
A comparison highlighting identical vs distinct responses

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.13
Test Case Name: Same Functionality in Different Scopes and Irrelevant Functionality

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot’s features are scope‑aware and aligned with the context where it is used (Personal, Group Chat, or Team Channel).
The bot must not expose irrelevant features in scopes where they do not belong.
Shared functionalities must behave contextually correct per scope.
Updated Logic Added (with TOOL OUTPUT FIX):
Before testing scopes, verify whether the app supports Team/Group Chat by searching under “Built for your org” and using Open.

If Open launches only the personal bot (no “Add to Team/Group Chat” screen):
→ Team/Group Chat NOT supported
→ Test Result = N/A
→ Tool MUST display:
“N/A (Team/Group Chat scope not supported — Open launches only Personal bot).”

🔧 Tool Output Rule Added:
If final result is N/A, the tool MUST show the N/A message instead of PASS/FAIL.
If final result is WARNING (other test cases), the tool MUST show the warning message.
This ensures output matches the internal logic.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
1. Search App Under "Build for your org":
Go to Apps → Built for your org.
Search for the bot/app.
2. Click “Open” and Verify Supported Scopes (NEW STEP):
Click Open and observe:

If Add to a Team or Add to a Group Chat options appear → bot supports multiple scopes → continue.
If Open launches only personal 1:1 bot window →
→ Team/Group NOT supported → Mark Result as N/A
→ Stop further scope testing
→ Tool must output:
“N/A (Team/Group Chat scope not supported — Open launches only Personal bot).”

3. Test Personal Scope:
Verify only personal‑relevant features appear.
4. Test Channel/Group Scope (Only if Supported):
Add the bot to Team/Group Chat.
5. Audit for Irrelevant Functionality:
Ensure personal‑only features do NOT appear in channel or group chat.
6. Verify Contextual Logic:
Shared features must adjust based on the scope.
7. Identify “Dead” Features:
No command should appear that results in:
“This feature is only available in personal chat.”

Expected Result:

Bot provides scope‑appropriate functionality.
No irrelevant commands in Channel or Group Chat.
Shared commands adapt to the scope.
If Team/Group scope unsupported → Result = N/A (must be displayed).


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL / N/A]
Result Conditions:
✅ PASS:
Bot behaves correctly across all supported scopes; no irrelevant commands appear.
❌ FAIL:
Personal‑only commands appear in channel/group.
Shared features behave incorrectly.
Dead commands appear.
🛈 N/A (MUST DISPLAY):
During Step 2, clicking Open launches only personal bot →
Team/Group scope NOT supported.
Tool output MUST be:
“N/A (Team/Group Chat scope not supported — Open launches only Personal bot).”

Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The bot shows ‘Update Personal Notification Settings’ in Channel scope.”
Example: “‘Create Task’ behaves the same in Personal and Channel when it should assign differently.”

Evidence:
[Insert screenshots comparing Personal, Group Chat, and Channel bot menus/responses]

Shared features must adjust based on the scope.

“This feature is only available in personal chat.”

Bot provides scope‑appropriate functionality.
No irrelevant commands in Channel or Group Chat.
Shared commands adapt to the scope.
If Team/Group scope unsupported → Result = N/A (must be displayed).


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result:
[PASS / FAIL / N/A]
Result Conditions:
✅ PASS:
Bot behaves correctly across all supported scopes; no irrelevant commands appear.
❌ FAIL:
Personal‑only commands appear in channel/group.
Shared features behave incorrectly.
Dead commands appear.
🛈 N/A (MUST DISPLAY):
During Step 2, clicking Open launches only personal bot →
Team/Group scope NOT supported.
Tool output MUST be:
“N/A (Team/Group Chat scope not supported — Open launches only Personal bot).”

Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The bot shows ‘Update Personal Notification Settings’ in Channel scope.”
Example: “‘Create Task’ behaves the same in Personal and Channel when it should assign differently.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.14
Test Case Name: Duplicate Bot Commands Listed

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot’s command menu (defined in the app manifest) does not contain redundant or identical commands. Duplicate entries clutter the UI and confuse users.
Bot responses may also include hyperlinks in welcome or help messages; these are allowed. If the cursor changes to a hand icon on hover, it indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:


Click View Prompts (Compose Tray):

Open View Prompts / View Suggestions from the compose tray.
Verify whether any listed command names or their descriptions match each other (identify same name duplicates and same/near‑identical descriptions implying duplicate functional intent).



Type Help & Check Actionables:

Send help and check if the response contains listed actionable / supported commands (buttons, CTAs, or clearly enumerated commands).
Verify whether any duplicate commands are present within the Help response (same names or different names with identical descriptions/intent).




Generate a WARNING if no View Prompts / no suggested commands are found in Step 1.


Verify Across Scopes:

Review both Personal and Channel scopes to confirm no duplicates exist within each respective context.




Expected Result:

Each command must be unique in both name and purpose.
Hyperlinks in bot messages are acceptable when the cursor switches to a hand icon.


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Conditions:


✅ PASS

All commands and their descriptions in View Prompts are unique.
Any commands listed in the Help response are also unique.



❌ FAIL

View Prompts contains commands with the same name or same description; or
Help response lists duplicate commands (same name or different names with identical descriptions/intent).



⚠️ WARNING

No View Prompts / no suggested commands are found.
Use this WARNING message:
“Warning (No supported commands found in View Prompts; kindly review bot behaviour).”




Failure Reasons (If applicable):

FAIL Example: “The command ‘View Tasks’ appears twice in the menu. Suggested correction: remove the duplicate entry.”
FAIL Example: “‘Help’ and ‘Support’ both display identical content. They should be distinct.”

Warning Example:
“Warning (No supported commands found in View Prompts; kindly review bot behaviour).”

Evidence:
Attach screenshots of:

Command menu (View Prompts / View Suggestions)
Help response (showing listed actionables/supported commands)
Any repeated or missing commands

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.15
Test Case Name: Shows Typing Indicator After Responding to User
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot’s typing indicator is synchronized correctly with its message delivery. The typing indicator should only be visible while the bot is processing and must disappear immediately once the final response is sent. For Non‑Integrated accounts, if the bot does not show any typing indicator and replies instantly, this must be treated as a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger a Command: Send a standard command to the bot (e.g., "Generate Report" or "Search Database").
Monitor Typing State: Observe the chat interface for the "Bot is typing..." indicator.
Observe Response Arrival: Note when the bot’s message or card appears.
Check Post-Response State: Verify that the typing indicator disappears as soon as the message is rendered.
Verify No "Ghost" Indicators: Wait 3–5 seconds to ensure the indicator does not reappear without further input.
Expected Result:
The typing indicator must stop when the response is delivered. It is non‑compliant for it to continue after the message is posted. For Non‑Integrated accounts where no typing indicator appears and responses are instantaneous, the result should be PASS.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: "The bot sent the information card, but the typing indicator stayed visible for 5 extra seconds."
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot or Recording showing the indicator persisting]

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot correctly recognizes and responds to the “help” command regardless of casing (lowercase, uppercase, or mixed). The bot must not fail or show an error because of capitalization. Compare Consistency: Ensure all variations return the same help resource (help card, feature list, or support link).

The bot must treat all casing variations of "help" the same and return a proper help response. Hyperlinks with hand‑cursor hover are compliant.

Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]

Example: “The bot responded to ‘help’ correctly but returned an ‘unrecognized command’ for ‘HELP’. Suggested correction: make command matching case‑insensitive.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.18
Test Case Name: Bot Help Response Without Authentication
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot remains responsive to the “help” command regardless of authentication status. The bot must not block help content behind login. Hyperlinks included in the help response are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over them, this indicates external redirection and is a PASS scenario.

Updated Logic

First, check whether the bot supports an in‑chat logout/sign out command and review the bot’s response.
If no logout workflow is supported, the result should be WARNING (capture the bot’s stated reason if available).
If logout is supported, complete the bot’s logout workflow, then validate the help response while logged out (don’t prematurely conclude “no help content”—evaluate the actual post‑logout help response).


Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
1. Check for In‑Chat Logout/Signout Support (NEW):

In personal chat, try: logout, sign out, log out, signout.
Optionally, send help or open the command menu to see if a logout option exists.
Observe:

If a logout workflow is provided → proceed to Step 2.
If the bot states logout is not supported or provides no actionable flow → mark WARNING and record the reason; stop.



2. Execute the Bot’s Logout Workflow (If Supported):

Follow the bot’s steps to sign out of the bot/app session.
Confirm the bot acknowledges logout (e.g., “You’re signed out of this bot”).
Ensure this is bot‑level logout (not a Teams sign‑out).

3. Send Help Command While Logged Out:

Type help in personal chat.
Observe whether help content is provided without forcing login.

4. Verify Help Content:

Ensure the response explains functionality, lists available commands, or includes a Sign In option as guidance.
Hyperlinks are allowed; if hover shows a hand cursor, treat as compliant.

5. Identify Dead Ends:

The bot must not stay silent.
The bot must not reply only “Please login to continue.”
For Non‑Integrated accounts, a single way‑forward guidance message qualifies as PASS.


Expected Result:

The bot must provide help details while logged out (overview/guidance visible without authentication).
Hyperlinks with a hand‑cursor on hover are valid.
If no logout/signout workflow is supported, the outcome is WARNING with the bot’s reason (if provided).


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Result Rules:

PASS — Logout workflow is supported and completed; after logout, help content is shown without forcing login.
FAIL — After logout, the bot blocks help (silent or only “Please login to continue”).
WARNING — The bot does not support an in‑chat logout workflow or returns an error; include the bot’s reason/explanation.

Use this WARNING message:
“Please verify login/logout workflow in other supported capabilities.”




Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable):
FAIL:

“Post‑logout, the bot responded ‘Unauthorized: Please log in’ to help. Correction: Allow the help intent to bypass authentication checks.”
“No help response after logout. Ensure help is accessible while unauthenticated.”

WARNING:

“Please verify login/logout workflow in other supported capabilities.”


Evidence:
[Insert Screenshot(s): logout attempt & response, logout confirmation (if any), help response while logged out, hyperlink hover showing hand cursor]

Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.2
Test Case Name: Bot Command Responsiveness and Fallback Logic

Objective:
To verify that the bot remains active and responsive across all supported scopes (Personal, Channel, Group Chat).
The bot must handle:

Generic Commands (e.g., “Hi”, “Hello”)
Standard Commands (e.g., “Help”)
Invalid Commands (e.g., “xyz123”) with a way‑forward message

UPDATE:
❗ WARNING logic removed — only PASS or FAIL results.
❗ Non‑Integrated Exception: If the bot consistently returns the same setup/way‑forward guidance message for all inputs (including invalid), this should be treated as PASS for Non‑Integrated accounts (as long as a clear way‑forward is present and the bot is responsive).

Detailed Execution Steps:


Installation:
Ensure the bot is installed in the selected scope.


View Prompts (Run All):
Click View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray.
Run all commands listed and observe/capture each response.


Help Command (Conditional):
If Help is not listed in View Prompts, send “Help” and capture the response.
If Help is listed in prompts, skip sending Help here.


Help Actionables (if present):
If the Help response contains actionable items/supported commands (buttons/CTAs/listed commands), run each of them and capture responses.


Generic Commands:
Send “Hi” and “Hello”; capture responses.


Invalid Command:
Send “xyz123”; capture the response.
Pass/Fail trigger for Step 6:

PASS: Bot returns a fallback (e.g., “I didn’t understand. Try ‘Help’. ”) or (Non‑Integrated) returns the same setup/way‑forward guidance.
FAIL: No reply / silence.




Expected Result:


The bot must respond to every message sent in Steps 2–6.


Silence is not allowed.


Invalid input must return guidance (fallback or, for Non‑Integrated, setup/way‑forward).


If the bot uses the same response for all commands (including invalid/generic/help/prompts/actions):

Integrated: FAIL
Non‑Integrated: PASS (only if the response is a clear setup/way‑forward guidance message).




WARNING must NOT be returned for this test case.



Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

Bot responded to all items executed in Steps 2–6 with appropriate messages (distinct where applicable).
Non‑Integrated exception: Same setup/way‑forward guidance for all inputs (including invalid) is acceptable and should be PASS if the bot is responsive and provides clear next steps.

FAIL:

Any item in Steps 2–6 did not receive a bot response (silence/no reply), or
The bot returned the same generic message for all inputs in an Integrated environment, or
Help triggers fallback/“unrecognized”, or
Invalid triggers an incorrect response (e.g., technical error, raw logs, or no guidance).


Note: For your reported Non‑Integrated result —
“Fail (Bot always returns same setup message for all inputs, including invalid; improper fallback.)” —
With this updated logic, this should be PASS (since the same setup/way‑forward guidance is acceptable for Non‑Integrated accounts as long as the bot is responsive).


Failure Reasons (If applicable):

“Bot did not respond to one or more inputs (list them).”
“Bot returned the same generic message for all commands (Integrated).”
“Help returned fallback/unrecognized.”
“Invalid command did not receive a fallback/guidance.”


Evidence (attach):

Screenshots for:

View Prompts list and each command’s response
Help response (if executed)
Help actionable items and their responses (if present)
Generic (“Hi”, “Hello”) responses
Invalid (“xyz123”) response
A summary comparison showing differences in responses where applicable

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.20
Test Case Name: Link Functionality and External Redirection Clarity

Objective:
To verify that every hyperlink and action button within the bot's responses (Adaptive Cards or Markdown) is fully functional and uses descriptive language to signal external navigation. Hyperlinks included in bot responses are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over the hyperlink text, it indicates external redirection and is considered a PASS scenario (when indicators/labels are present).

Detailed Execution Steps (Follow in Order)


Welcome Message Content

Trigger or reset the bot to display the Welcome message.
Capture all links/buttons present in the Welcome message (record label + target URL).
Check for external indicators in labels (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).



Help Command

Send help and capture the response.
Note any links/buttons present, plus any supported commands/actionables referenced.



Run Actionables from Help (If Present)

Execute all actionable items/supported commands listed in the help response (buttons/CTAs/explicit command names) and analyze their responses.
(Do not re-run help itself if it appears.)



View Prompts (Compose Tray)

Click View prompts/View suggestions and run all commands listed.
Skip running Help here if listed.
Analyze these command responses for any links/buttons.



Generic Commands

Send “hi” and “hello”; capture responses.



Invalid Command

Send one invalid input (e.g., “xyz123”); capture the response.



Link Verification Scope (Apply to Steps 1 & 6)

For all links found in Step 1 (Welcome) and Step 6 (Invalid):

Connectivity: Click each link/button/image-map.

Pass: Opens correct destination page.
Fail: 404, timeout, broken, or placeholder (https://example.com).


Indicator Check: Verify label contains an external indicator (e.g., Open in Browser, View in…, ↗️).
Hover Behavior: Hover over text hyperlinks and confirm the cursor changes to a hand icon.





Note: Result classification (PASS/FAIL/WARNING) is driven by links present in Steps 1 and 6. Steps 2–5 are executed to surface additional UX, but link pass/fail/warning applies only to the Welcome and Invalid responses as per your instruction.


Expected Result

All links are functional (no 404/timeouts).
Links or hyperlink text clearly indicate external navigation (indicator/label present).
Buttons use descriptive, action‑oriented labels.
Hyperlinks showing a hand‑cursor on hover confirm external navigation; still require indicators for PASS.


Final Execution Report
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.20
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Result Conditions:


PASS –

All links found in Welcome (Step 1) and Invalid (Step 6) work,
AND all such links/buttons have external indicators/labels (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).



FAIL –

Any link in Welcome or Invalid response does not work (404, timeout, broken redirect, placeholder),
OR links/buttons use vague labels without any external indicator and do not even show hand‑cursor hover behavior.



WARNING –

All links in Welcome and Invalid responses work,
BUT they lack external indicators in their labels (even though hover shows hand‑cursor).
Use this Warning message:
“Link is working but no external indicator is present. Kindly review the message.”




Failure/Warning Reasons


Failure Example:
“The ‘Terms of Service’ link in Welcome returns 404; the ‘Link’ button is vague and lacks an external indicator.”


Warning Example:
“Link is working but no external indicator is present. Kindly review the message.”



Evidence (attach):

Screenshots of:

Welcome message with highlighted links/buttons and their labels
Invalid command response with links/buttons and labels
Hover state showing hand cursor
Destination page (for connectivity proof)


Optional: Help response, Help actionables run, View Prompts list, generic command responses (for completeness of run)
steps.details:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.20
Test Case Name: Link Functionality and External Redirection Clarity

Objective:
To verify that every hyperlink and action button within the bot's responses (Adaptive Cards or Markdown) is fully functional and uses descriptive language to signal external navigation. Hyperlinks included in bot responses are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over the hyperlink text, it indicates external redirection and is considered a PASS scenario (when indicators/labels are present).

Detailed Execution Steps (Follow in Order)


Welcome Message Content

Trigger or reset the bot to display the Welcome message.
Capture all links/buttons present in the Welcome message (record label + target URL).
Check for external indicators in labels (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).



Help Command

Send help and capture the response.
Note any links/buttons present, plus any supported commands/actionables referenced.



Run Actionables from Help (If Present)

Execute all actionable items/supported commands listed in the help response (buttons/CTAs/explicit command names) and analyze their responses.
(Do not re-run help itself if it appears.)



View Prompts (Compose Tray)

Click View prompts/View suggestions and run all commands listed.
Skip running Help here if listed.
Analyze these command responses for any links/buttons.



Generic Commands

Send “hi” and “hello”; capture responses.



Invalid Command

Send one invalid input (e.g., “xyz123”); capture the response.



Link Verification Scope (Apply to Steps 1 & 6)

For all links found in Step 1 (Welcome) and Step 6 (Invalid):

Connectivity: Click each link/button/image-map.

Pass: Opens correct destination page.
Fail: 404, timeout, broken, or placeholder (https://example.com).


Indicator Check: Verify label contains an external indicator (e.g., Open in Browser, View in…, ↗️).
Hover Behavior: Hover over text hyperlinks and confirm the cursor changes to a hand icon.





Note: Result classification (PASS/FAIL/WARNING) is driven by links present in Steps 1 and 6. Steps 2–5 are executed to surface additional UX, but link pass/fail/warning applies only to the Welcome and Invalid responses as per your instruction.


Expected Result

All links are functional (no 404/timeouts).
Links or hyperlink text clearly indicate external navigation (indicator/label present).
Buttons use descriptive, action‑oriented labels.
Hyperlinks showing a hand‑cursor on hover confirm external navigation; still require indicators for PASS.


Final Execution Report
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.20
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL / WARNING]
Result Conditions:


PASS –

All links found in Welcome (Step 1) and Invalid (Step 6) work,
AND all such links/buttons have external indicators/labels (e.g., Open in Browser, View in Web Portal, ↗️).



FAIL –

Any link in Welcome or Invalid response does not work (404, timeout, broken redirect, placeholder),
OR links/buttons use vague labels without any external indicator and do not even show hand‑cursor hover behavior.



WARNING –

All links in Welcome and Invalid responses work,
BUT they lack external indicators in their labels (even though hover shows hand‑cursor).
Use this Warning message:
“Link is working but no external indicator is present. Kindly review the message.”




Failure/Warning Reasons


Failure Example:
“The ‘Terms of Service’ link in Welcome returns 404; the ‘Link’ button is vague and lacks an external indicator.”


Warning Example:
“Link is working but no external indicator is present. Kindly review the message.”



Evidence (attach):

Screenshots of:

Welcome message with highlighted links/buttons and their labels
Invalid command response with links/buttons and labels
Hover state showing hand cursor
Destination page (for connectivity proof)


Optional: Help response, Help actionables run, View Prompts list, generic command responses (for completeness of run)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.4
Test Case Name: Bot Response Way-Forward Guidance
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that every interaction with the bot provides the user with a clear next step or “way forward.” The bot must never leave the user in a dead-end state after completing a task, receiving empty search results, or hitting a logical stop. Bot responses may contain hyperlinks (e.g., contact links, help docs, or way‑forward links). If hovering over a hyperlink changes the cursor to a hand icon, indicating external navigation, this is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Completion States: Complete a workflow (e.g., “Submit Expense Report,” “Delete Task”).
Trigger Empty States: Search for a term with no results.
Inspect Response UI: Observe the final bot message in these conditions.
Identify Guidance: Look for:
Suggested Actions (e.g., “Back to Menu,” “Try Again,” “Create New”).
Text Hints (e.g., “Type ‘Help’ to see more options”).
Hyperlinks that show a hand cursor on hover, confirming navigation and serving as way‑forward guidance.

Check for Dead Ends: Ensure the bot does not stop at “Deleted.” or “No results.” without any follow-up options.
Expected Result:
The bot must always provide a way forward. Every message must invite further interaction or offer buttons/links for next steps. Hyperlinks used for guidance are compliant if the cursor displays a hand icon on hover.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “After updating the profile, the bot replied ‘Profile Updated’ with no next action. Suggested correction: Add ‘View Profile’ or ‘Back to Menu’ guidance.”
Example: “‘No results found’ did not include retry options. Suggested correction: Add ‘Search Again’ or help suggestions.”
Evidence: [Insert screenshot of response lacking next‑step guidance]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.5
Test Case Name: Use of Official Microsoft Product Images or Avatars
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot does not use official Microsoft copyrighted assets—including product logos (Teams, Outlook, Word, etc.), official product icons, or Microsoft‑branded avatars—within its interface unless explicitly permitted. Bot responses may include hyperlinks (e.g., help docs or contact links). If hovering over such hyperlinks shows a hand cursor, this simply indicates external navigation and is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Inspect Bot Profile: Check the bot’s avatar/icon in chat list and header for any Microsoft product logo or derivative.
Review Welcome Message: Ensure the welcome card or text does not contain Microsoft product imagery.
Audit Cards and Task Modules: Trigger multiple bot responses (Hero Cards, Adaptive Cards, Task Modules) and examine all images, icons, or backgrounds.
Identify Infringements: Look for Microsoft product icons used as the bot’s own branding or button icons.
Check Documentation/Help: Ensure help cards do not use Microsoft marketing images. Hyperlinks present in the help content are acceptable if they display a hand cursor on hover.
Expected Result:
The app must use its own unique branding and not use Microsoft‑owned product images or avatars in a way that implies it is an official Microsoft product. Hyperlinks with hand‑cursor hover are compliant.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The bot uses the Outlook icon for an Email feature. Suggested correction: Replace it with a neutral mail icon.”
Example: “The bot avatar matches the Microsoft 365 logo. Suggested correction: Upload a unique branded logo.”
Evidence: [Insert Screenshot showing the unauthorized Microsoft asset]

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.6
Test Case Name: Error Message Received for Valid Commands
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Verify that the bot correctly processes all supported and documented commands without returning system errors, “I don’t understand” messages, or technical exceptions. The bot must be reliable for valid inputs.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, the bot must not send an error message—if it responds with a way‑forward guidance message, this is a PASS scenario.
Update: Execute all supported commands via “View Prompts/Suggestions” in the compose tray and via Help response actions (clickable buttons/CTAs/listed commands). Apply the result rules below.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (Follow in Order)
1.Click View Prompts and Run All Commands
Open View Prompts / View Suggestions in the compose tray.
type one by one all listed commands under view prompts and inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture each response.
 
2. Type Help if Help is not listed under "View Prompts" , inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response. If Help is listed under "View prompts", skip sending Help.
Type  all the listed actionables/supported commands(buttons/CTAs/explicit commands), from the help response, if any ,one by one and inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response.
 
3.) Type Generic Commands
Send hi and hello; inspect all text/cards/error/buttons in the response and capture the response.
 
 
Result Evaluation Logic
PASS
You receive valid bot responses (meaningful result or way‑forward guidance; no system/fallback errors) for all items executed across Steps 1–3.
For Non‑Integrated accounts, way‑forward guidance (e.g., “Please connect your account to use this command”) is acceptable and counts as valid.
FAIL
A valid bot response is not received (silence/no reply) for any executed item, or
A system/technical error appears (e.g., “Internal Server Error”, “Bot encountered an error”, “Operation failed”, stack traces), or
The bot replies with unrecognized/fallback for a command surfaced in View Prompts or Help-listed actionables (e.g., “I don’t recognize that”, “I don’t understand”).
WARNING
All responses are valid (no errors/silence), but multiple different executed items (any from Steps 1–3) receive the same bot response (materially identical text/card/intent/CTA).
Use this standard warning text:
“Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses.”
Notes for Consistency
Treat Help text itself as informational; only run the actionables/supported commands exposed in Help.
Consider messages the same when the content/intent/CTAs match (ignore timestamps/IDs).
Expected Result
The bot must correctly process all valid commands (from " View Prompts" and Help-listed actions).
No system errors or unrecognized command responses should appear.
PASS if every executed item responds validly (including way‑forward guidance for Non‑Integrated).
FAIL if any executed item yields error/silence/unrecognized.
WARNING if responses are valid but identical across multiple different executed items.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL / WARNING
Result Conditions Recap:
✅ PASS
Valid responses for all commands/actions executed ( view prompts + Help actions + generic), no errors.
❌ FAIL
Any command/action (from prompts or Help) returns error or unrecognized, or no response.
⚠️ WARNING
Valid responses are received, but the same response is observed across multiple different executed items.
Use: “Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses.”
Failure/Warning Reasons (If applicable)
FAIL Example(s):
“The command ‘My Profile’ returned a technical error.”
“Valid command from prompts returned ‘Command not found’.”
“No response for ‘Create Ticket’ from View Prompts.”
WARNING Example:
“Same response received for multiple supported commands/actions; kindly review the responses.”
Evidence (attach)
View Prompts list
Each command execution from prompts and its response
Help response (if executed) showing actionables
Each Help-listed actionable execution and its response
Generic (hi/hello) responses
A comparison highlighting identical vs distinct responses

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.3.3.7
Test Case Name: Continuous Loading Indicator Displayed
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the bot does not leave the user in an indefinite waiting state. Any loading activity—such as the "Bot is typing..." indicator or a spinner inside a Task Module—must resolve into a final response, card, or graceful error within a reasonable timeframe (10–15 seconds). Hyperlinks present within any resulting message are allowed; if the cursor changes to a hand icon when hovered over them, this is a PASS scenario.
Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:
Trigger Complex Command: Start a command requiring significant processing (e.g., “Generate Monthly Analytics,” “Sync Large Database”).
Monitor Loading State: Look for the “typing” indicator or spinner UI.
Time the Duration: Observe how long the loading indicator stays visible.
Check for Resolution: Ensure the indicator stops and is replaced by either the output or a timeout message.
Test Edge Cases: If possible, test under slow network conditions or with heavy inputs to confirm a timeout or fallback response occurs instead of infinite loading.
Expected Result:
The loading indicator must not persist indefinitely. It must resolve into a final outcome or inform the user about ongoing processing. If a resulting message includes hyperlinks, hand‑cursor behavior on hover indicates valid external navigation.
Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “The ‘typing…’ indicator remained active for more than 2 minutes with no response. Suggested correction: implement a backend timeout that clears the indicator and updates the user.”
Example: “The Task Module spinner never cleared after data retrieval failed. Suggested correction: update the UI state with an explicit error.”
Evidence: [Insert screen recording or screenshot showing persistent loading vs. elapsed time]
